station.title: Contraception, learning disability, enzyme-inducing AED
station.candidate_instructions: Sophie Wright, 20, mild to moderate learning disability. Epilepsy controlled on Carbamazepine 200mg BD. Attends alone requesting the pill.
station.station_script: You are Sophie Wright, 20, with a mild learning disability. You want to go on the pill because you have a boyfriend, Liam (21). You do not want a baby. You take two little white epilepsy tablets a day that your mum puts out; you sometimes forget the evening one. You and Liam have not had sex yet. You do not want your mum told.
station.data_gathering: Open agenda setting; sensitive coercion and safeguarding screen; ICE; adherence difficulty; explore epilepsy and medication; sexual and menstrual history; capacity assessed conversationally.
station.clinical_management: Recognise Carbamazepine is an enzyme inducer making the COCP and standard POP unreliable; steer to a long acting method unaffected by enzyme induction (injection or intrauterine); offer accessible written information; specific follow up; handle confidentiality.
station.relating_to_others: Clear, age respecting language; reassure confidentiality for a capacitous adult; check understanding (teach back); offer accessible information; acknowledge relationship pressure.
station.clinical_learning_points: Carbamazepine is a strong enzyme inducer: the combined pill and the standard progestogen only pill are unreliable. The implant is also reduced by enzyme inducers and is not first line. Methods unaffected by induction: the injection (DMPA) and intrauterine methods (copper IUD or the levonorgestrel IUS). Capacity for the specific decision should be assessed conversationally, not as a formal test; that would be disempowering. Adults with a learning disability are at higher risk of exploitation, so a coercion screen is essential. Offer Easy Read information as a reasonable adjustment.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Hello, I am Dr Okafor, one of the GPs. Can I check I am speaking with Sophie Wright, and your date of birth?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yes that is me. I came by myself today. I want to go on the pill. My friend Sarah is on it and she said I need it now because I have a boyfriend.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Thank you for coming in to talk about it. Tell me a bit about what is going on.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: I have got a boyfriend now, Liam, and we want to, you know, do more soon. I do not want a baby.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Tell me about Liam, how did you two meet?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: At college. He is 21. He is really nice, he buys me chips.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Has he ever made you do anything you did not want to?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: No, nothing like that. We just kiss right now. We talked about doing more, we both want to.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: What is it about the pill that you were hoping for?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: I definitely do not want a baby. My sister just had one and she looks so tired all the time. The pill stops babies, right?

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: It can do. Can I ask about your epilepsy, how is that at the moment?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Good. I have not had a fit for ages, more than a year. Mum says so.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: And the tablets for it?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Two a day, one breakfast one dinner. Mum puts them out for me. Little white ones, I do not know the name.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: If you had a pill to take every day yourself, do you think you could remember it?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: I do not know. If Mum is not there I forget the dinner one sometimes. Sometimes I forget to brush my teeth.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Have you and Liam had sex yet?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: No, not yet. Just kissing. But we want to soon.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: Do your periods come regularly?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yeah, every month, about four or five days, not too heavy.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: I think the best thing is the combined pill. I will start you on that today.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Okay, thank you. I will take it when I remember.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: It is very safe. Use condoms too if you can.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Does Mum need to know? Please do not tell her, she thinks I am still a little girl.

transcript.speaker: candidate
transcript.text: It is confidential, I will not tell her. Come back if you have any problems.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Okay. Thank you Doctor.
